data.sentence: Hi Brianna, thanks for making time today — I know you're probably slammed this week.
Yeah, honestly it's been a rough couple of weeks, but, um, we needed to have this conversation so here we are.
I totally understand, and I appreciate you being direct about that — I'd rather we just get into it than dance around things.
I also pulled in Leo from our security engineering team because honestly he's the one who's been living in your platform day to day and he has, uh, a lot of thoughts.
Hey Leo, good to have you on — I've seen your support tickets come through and I definitely want to make sure we address what you've been seeing.
Yeah, hi Maria. I'll be honest, I've been pretty frustrated. Like, the past week especially has been... not great.
So let's start there — can you walk me through what's been happening from your perspective? I want to hear it directly from you.

Okay so, the outage. March tenth through the eleventh. We had a six-hour window where Aegis Detect just... went dark. No alerts, no visibility, nothing. And we're a tech company, we have compliance obligations, we have customers who depend on us being secure — that's not a minor inconvenience.
And what made it worse honestly was that we didn't even find out from Aegis. We noticed it ourselves because our dashboards just stopped updating. Like, how does that happen?

You're right, and I'm not going to sugarcoat it — that was a significant failure on our part, both the outage itself and the communication lag. We should have been proactively reaching out to you within the first hour.

I mean, we got the postmortem email a few days later, which was fine, but like... six hours is a really long time to be flying blind from a threat monitoring standpoint. Anything could have happened in that window.
And look, I don't want to be overdramatic about it, but it does make us question the reliability of the platform as a whole. If this happened once, what's to say it doesn't happen again?

That's a completely fair question and I want to give you a real answer on that, not just reassurances. The root cause was a single point of failure in the event ingestion pipeline — essentially one component failed and it cascaded. The engineering team has since implemented redundant processing nodes and a circuit breaker pattern, so that specific failure mode is addressed.

Okay but how do we know that? Like, I appreciate the explanation but we're being asked to renew a contract and our confidence in the infrastructure is shaken. Do you have documentation on the remediation? Something we can actually review?

Yes, absolutely — I can get you the full technical remediation report, and I can also set up a call with our engineering team if that would be helpful so you can ask questions directly. I think that would be more valuable than just a document.
That could be useful, yeah. Leo, would you want to do that?

I mean, yeah, I'd want to dig into the architecture changes. But I still think the broader question is around SLAs. Like, what is our actual SLA for Detect uptime and were we compensated for that breach?

So your current contract has a 99.5% uptime SLA for Detect, and the six-hour outage did breach that. I'm going to be honest with you — I've been working with our team on a service credit for the affected period, and I have something concrete to bring to you today.
Okay, I mean, that's something at least. What are we talking?

I want to frame it in the context of the overall renewal conversation rather than just throwing a number out, because I think there's a broader package we should discuss. But yes, there's a meaningful credit on the table.
Okay, fair enough. Let's talk about the renewal then, because there's more to it than just the outage. We've also been, um, we've been doing some evaluations of other options in the market.
I'd expect nothing less, honestly. Can you tell me more about what you've been looking at?

We've been talking to SentinelShield. They reached out to us actually, right around the time the outage happened — which, great timing on their part. And we took the meeting because, you know, we felt like we should at least understand our options.
That makes sense. What's been their pitch to you?

They're aggressive on pricing, I'll say that. And they're making a lot of claims about uptime guarantees — 99.9% SLA on their detection product. Which, after what we just went through, that's an appealing number.
Yeah, and they have a... I don't want to overstate it, but they have a slick demo. We've only done one call so we're early in that conversation. But Brianna, you want to tell her the other stuff?

Right, so, the other thing is we've been feeling like the platform as a whole isn't growing fast enough for where we're headed. We're scaling up, we're going to have some significant compliance requirements in the next twelve months — SOC 2, we're looking at HIPAA as we move into a new vertical — and we need a platform that can grow with us.

Okay, so that's actually a really important thing for me to flag here, because this is directly relevant. Aegis Comply v2 just launched — well, it's going live next month, April seventh — and it has on-demand reporting for SOC 2, HIPAA, PCI DSS, and ISO 27001 all in one place. That's new functionality that directly addresses what you're describing.
Wait, when does that launch? Because we've been on Comply and honestly it's felt a little stagnant. Like the reporting has been clunky.

April seventh is the GA date. I can get you access to the release notes and if you want, we can get you into an early preview or at least a demo of v2 before renewal conversations close. The on-demand piece is a big deal — you're not waiting for scheduled reports, you can pull a SOC 2 report whenever an auditor asks.
That would actually be helpful to see. Bri, that's kind of the thing we were complaining about last month, right? Having to wait for the reporting cycle.
Yeah, it is. I mean, I don't want to get too excited about something that's not live yet — we've been burned before with roadmap promises. But okay, I'd like to see a demo.

Totally fair, and I hear you on the roadmap skepticism — I'll make sure what I show you is real and shipping, not vaporware. So let me make sure I understand everything on the table: the outage and infrastructure trust, the SLA credit, the competitive evaluation with SentinelShield, and the compliance roadmap for the next year. Did I miss anything?
Pricing. Our budget for next year is tighter than it was when we signed. We need the renewal to reflect that reality.
Okay. And where does the renewal land relative to your budget right now — like, are we talking ten percent, twenty percent, is there a number you're working against?

We need to come down meaningfully on the total. I don't want to throw out a number yet because I want to see what you put together first. But SentinelShield is significantly cheaper for what looks like comparable coverage.

I want to make sure we're comparing apples to apples there, because sometimes what looks comparable in a demo is missing pieces — especially around the identity and backup modules which I know you rely on. But I'll build something out. Can I ask what your renewal deadline is internally?
Our contract is up end of April. So we're not in a panic, but we're not that far out either. And we'd want whatever's next decided by, I don't know, first week of April so legal has time.

Okay, that gives us a couple weeks which is tight but workable. Here's what I'm going to do — I'll put together a renewal proposal that includes the SLA credit, a pricing option that reflects your budget situation, and I'll bundle in access to Comply v2. And I'll also coordinate the engineering call for Leo. Does that feel like a reasonable next step?
Yeah, that's reasonable. I'd want to see that by end of this week if possible so we have time to react.
I can commit to Thursday. Leo, any final things from your end that you want to make sure are in scope?

Just the engineering call on the remediation. That's the thing that'll determine for me whether I recommend staying or not. The business case is Brianna's call but technically, I need to feel confident in the infrastructure before I can sign off on anything.

Understood. I'll set that up for early next week. Thank you both — I know this wasn't an easy conversation but I'd rather have it openly and I'm committed to getting you to a place where you feel good about where things are going.
Okay. We'll see what you come back with. Thanks Maria.
